subtopic_detail: Infective endocarditis surgical indications - heart failure with severe regurgitation
stem: A 52-year-old man with native aortic valve infective endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus is on day 5 of appropriate IV antibiotics. He was initially stable but has developed progressive dyspnoea and orthopnoea over 48 hours. Repeat echocardiography shows severe aortic regurgitation with a mobile 14 mm vegetation on the aortic valve. He has elevated JVP, bilateral basal crepitations, and a new early diastolic murmur.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108 bpm, BP 118/42 mmHg (wide pulse pressure), RR 28/min, SpO2 92% on 6 L face mask, T 37.8 C
data_table.Investigations: BNP 1,840 ng/L, Hb 108 g/L, WCC 16.2 x10^9/L, CRP 128 mg/L, blood cultures day 3: S. aureus (now on appropriate antibiotics)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue IV antibiotics for the full 6-week course and manage heart failure with IV diuretics only
options.rationale: New-onset heart failure from severe valvular regurgitation in the setting of active endocarditis is a class I indication for urgent surgical intervention. Medical management alone carries a very high mortality when heart failure develops. Waiting for antibiotic completion before surgery significantly worsens outcomes. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023; NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Switch to broader-spectrum antibiotics assuming treatment failure and reassess in 48 hours
options.rationale: The antibiotics are appropriate for S. aureus and there is no evidence of treatment failure in terms of microbiology. The deterioration is haemodynamic, caused by progressive valvular destruction leading to severe aortic regurgitation and heart failure. This is a mechanical complication requiring surgical intervention. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Commence IV sodium nitroprusside to reduce afterload and defer surgery until infection is controlled
options.rationale: While afterload reduction can provide temporary haemodynamic support, it is a bridging measure, not definitive treatment. Severe aortic regurgitation causing heart failure in endocarditis requires urgent surgery regardless of antibiotic duration. Delaying surgery until infection is cleared increases mortality. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023; NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Arrange percutaneous aortic valve replacement as a less invasive alternative to open surgery
options.rationale: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation is contraindicated in active endocarditis. The infected tissue requires surgical debridement and valve replacement under direct vision. Implanting a prosthesis into an infected field without debridement would seed the new valve. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Refer urgently for aortic valve replacement surgery while continuing IV antibiotics perioperatively
options.rationale: Heart failure from severe valvular regurgitation is the most common indication for surgery in infective endocarditis and carries a class I recommendation for urgent intervention. Surgery should not be delayed for antibiotic completion when there is haemodynamic deterioration. A 14 mm vegetation also meets size criteria for surgery (vegetation greater than 10 mm). IV antibiotics continue perioperatively and postoperatively. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023; NHFA/CSANZ)

explanation.summary: Infective endocarditis surgical indications are categorised as urgent (within 24 to 48 hours) or elective. The three main indications are heart failure from valvular dysfunction, uncontrolled infection (abscess, persistent bacteraemia, resistant organisms), and prevention of embolism (large vegetation greater than 10 mm, recurrent emboli). Heart failure is the strongest indication and should prompt surgery regardless of antibiotic duration. In S. aureus native valve endocarditis, early surgery within the first week has been associated with improved outcomes in selected patients.
key_points: Heart failure from valvular dysfunction is the most common and strongest surgical indication
Surgery should not be delayed to complete antibiotics when heart failure is present
Vegetation size greater than 10 mm is an additional surgical indication for embolic prevention
S. aureus endocarditis has high mortality and often requires early surgery
IV antibiotics continue perioperatively and for the full course postoperatively
explanation.pearls: The EARLY trial demonstrated that early surgery within 48 hours for left-sided endocarditis with large vegetations reduced the composite endpoint of death and embolic events at 6 months from 28% to 3% compared with conventional treatment - though this was a selected population without severe heart failure.

subtopic_detail: Infective endocarditis with perivalvular abscess and conduction abnormality
stem: A 48-year-old man with prosthetic aortic valve endocarditis caused by Enterococcus faecalis is on day 10 of IV ampicillin and gentamicin. He has been febrile intermittently despite appropriate antibiotics. His daily ECG today shows a new first-degree AV block with PR interval of 280 ms that was not present on admission. A transthoracic echocardiogram is technically limited due to prosthetic valve acoustic shadowing.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 68 bpm, BP 126/72 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, T 38.4 C
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus rhythm, PR 280 ms (admission PR 160 ms), WCC 14.8 x10^9/L, CRP 96 mg/L (down from 180 on admission), blood cultures day 8: sterile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Transoesophageal echocardiography to assess for perivalvular abscess and paravalvular leak
options.rationale: New conduction abnormality (first-degree AV block with PR prolongation from 160 to 280 ms) in prosthetic valve endocarditis is highly suggestive of perivalvular abscess extending to the conduction system. TOE is the investigation of choice as it has superior sensitivity (approximately 90%) for perivalvular complications compared to TTE (approximately 50%), especially with prosthetic valves where acoustic shadowing limits TTE. Perivalvular abscess is a surgical indication. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Cardiac MRI with gadolinium to characterise the myocardial tissue around the prosthetic valve
options.rationale: Cardiac MRI has limitations in the presence of prosthetic valves due to susceptibility artefacts from metallic components, which can obscure the perivalvular region. TOE is the first-line investigation for perivalvular complications. Cardiac CT may be considered if TOE is inconclusive. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram in 1 week as the TTE limitation is likely to resolve with fasting
options.rationale: The TTE limitation is due to prosthetic valve acoustic shadowing, which is a fixed physical limitation unrelated to patient preparation. Delaying investigation by a week when there is a new conduction abnormality suggesting abscess is dangerous. TOE is required for adequate assessment. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

options.text: 24-hour Holter monitor to characterise the degree of conduction disease before any further imaging
options.rationale: The clinical significance of this new AV block is not the conduction disease itself but what it implies about perivalvular infection. Holter monitoring will document the rhythm but will not diagnose the underlying abscess. The priority is TOE to identify the anatomical complication requiring surgery. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

options.text: Electrophysiology study to assess the level of AV block and need for permanent pacemaker insertion
options.rationale: The new AV block is caused by perivalvular abscess compressing the conduction system, not a primary conduction disorder. EPS is not indicated in this context. Surgical treatment of the abscess may resolve the conduction abnormality, and any pacing decision should follow definitive surgical management. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guidelines 2023)

explanation.summary: Perivalvular abscess is a serious complication of infective endocarditis that occurs in approximately 30 to 40% of prosthetic valve endocarditis cases. The aortic valve is most commonly affected due to the proximity of the aortic root to the AV node and His bundle. New conduction abnormalities (PR prolongation, bundle branch block, or complete heart block) are sentinel signs of abscess formation. TOE is the investigation of choice with approximately 90% sensitivity for perivalvular complications. Perivalvular abscess is an indication for urgent surgery regardless of antibiotic response.
key_points: New conduction abnormality in endocarditis suggests perivalvular abscess
TOE has approximately 90% sensitivity for perivalvular complications vs 50% for TTE
Prosthetic valve endocarditis has higher rates of perivalvular extension
Perivalvular abscess is a surgical indication regardless of microbiological response
The aortic root's proximity to the conduction system explains AV block development
explanation.pearls: Daily ECG monitoring during endocarditis treatment is essential - PR prolongation of more than 40 ms from baseline is the earliest sign of aortic root abscess extending to the conduction system and should trigger immediate TOE, even if the patient appears to be responding to antibiotics.

subtopic_detail: Delusional disorder recognition and initial management
stem: A 54-year-old woman is referred by her GP for psychiatric assessment. She has a fixed belief that her neighbour has been installing surveillance cameras in her home, which she has held for 8 months. She has called the police multiple times but no evidence has been found. She otherwise functions well at work, maintains friendships, and her behaviour is not obviously bizarre apart from issues relating to the belief about her neighbour. She has never experienced auditory hallucinations, thought disorder, or negative symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Well-groomed, cooperative, euthymic affect, fixed non-bizarre delusion of persecution, no hallucinations, no formal thought disorder, no negative symptoms, MMSE 30/30

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizophrenia with predominantly positive symptoms and preserved functioning
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires at least two of: delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, disorganised or catatonic behaviour, and negative symptoms, for a significant portion of one month. This patient has only a single isolated delusion without hallucinations, thought disorder, or negative symptoms, and her functioning is preserved. This pattern favours delusional disorder. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder, persecutory type, with preserved psychosocial functioning
options.rationale: Delusional disorder is characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions lasting at least 1 month, without prominent hallucinations, formal thought disorder, or negative symptoms. Functioning is relatively preserved apart from the impact of the delusion itself. The persecutory subtype involves conviction that one is being conspired against, spied on, or harassed. This patient meets all criteria: fixed persecutory delusion for 8 months, no other psychotic symptoms, preserved functioning. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with a persecutory delusion as the sole symptom feature
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder by definition lasts at least 1 day but less than 1 month, with full return to premorbid functioning. This patient's symptoms have persisted for 8 months, well beyond the 1-month threshold. The chronicity points to delusional disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Paranoid personality disorder with transient stress-related paranoid ideation
options.rationale: Paranoid personality disorder involves pervasive distrust and suspiciousness but the beliefs are typically overvalued ideas rather than fixed delusions. This patient has a fixed false belief that surveillance equipment has been installed, which is a frank delusion rather than personality-level suspiciousness. Additionally, the delusion is focused and specific rather than pervasive. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Late-onset schizophrenia given her age and isolated persecutory content
options.rationale: While late-onset schizophrenia (after age 40) can present with prominent persecutory delusions, it still requires the presence of at least two criterion A symptoms and is typically accompanied by auditory hallucinations. An isolated delusion without hallucinations or other psychotic features in a well-functioning individual is better classified as delusional disorder regardless of age of onset. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia; DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder is characterised by the presence of one or more non-bizarre delusions persisting for at least 1 month, in the absence of other prominent psychotic symptoms. Functioning is generally preserved except where directly related to the delusion. Subtypes include persecutory, jealous, erotomanic, grandiose, somatic, and mixed. Treatment involves antipsychotic medication (often low-dose) combined with CBT for psychosis, though engagement can be challenging as patients often lack insight. Prognosis is better than schizophrenia with many patients maintaining employment and social relationships.
key_points: Delusional disorder requires delusions lasting at least 1 month without other prominent psychotic symptoms
Functioning is relatively preserved apart from the direct impact of the delusion
Non-bizarre delusions involve situations that could theoretically occur in real life
Persecutory subtype is the most common form of delusional disorder
Treatment combines low-dose antipsychotic with CBT for psychosis
explanation.pearls: Delusional disorder has a lifetime prevalence of approximately 0.2% and typically presents in middle to late adulthood - the persecutory subtype accounts for approximately 50% of cases and can be distinguished from paranoid personality disorder by the presence of fixed false beliefs rather than pervasive suspiciousness.

subtopic_detail: Brief psychotic disorder recognition and recovery expectations
stem: A 26-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric history is brought to the emergency department by her family. Three days ago she experienced an acute onset of persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations, and disorganised speech following the sudden death of her mother. She has not slept in 3 days. Prior to this event she was functioning normally with no prodromal symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Agitated, disorganised speech with loose associations, persecutory delusions (believes hospital staff are poisoning her), auditory hallucinations (third-person commentary), no substance use on UDS, normal physical examination and bloods
data_table.Investigations: UDS negative, TFTs normal, FBC and UEC normal, CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the expected clinical course?

options.text: First-episode schizophrenia - expect chronic relapsing course requiring long-term antipsychotic therapy
options.rationale: While this presentation includes multiple criterion A symptoms of schizophrenia, the duration of only 3 days is too short for a schizophrenia diagnosis, which requires symptoms for at least 1 month (with at least 6 months of disturbance). The acute onset following a clear psychosocial stressor and absence of prodromal symptoms favour brief psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Acute stress disorder with psychotic features - treat with trauma-focused therapy alone
options.rationale: Acute stress disorder presents with dissociative, intrusive, avoidance, and arousal symptoms following trauma, but frank psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, formal thought disorder) are not part of the ASD diagnostic criteria. The presence of hallucinations and disorganised speech indicates a primary psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with marked stressor - expect full remission within 1 month of onset
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is characterised by sudden onset of at least one psychotic symptom (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, or grossly disorganised behaviour) lasting between 1 day and 1 month, with eventual full return to premorbid functioning. The specifier 'with marked stressor' applies when onset closely follows a significant psychosocial stressor. Short-term antipsychotic treatment is typically required during the acute episode, but the prognosis is good with expected full recovery. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder - expect resolution within 6 months with uncertain long-term prognosis
options.rationale: Schizophreniform disorder requires symptom duration of at least 1 month but less than 6 months. At only 3 days, the duration is too short for schizophreniform disorder. If symptoms persist beyond 1 month, the diagnosis would be revised. The acute onset with clear stressor and no prodrome makes brief psychotic disorder more likely. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Dissociative identity disorder presenting with hallucination-like experiences and identity disruption
options.rationale: Dissociative identity disorder involves disruption of identity with two or more distinct personality states and recurrent gaps in recall. While it can include hallucination-like experiences, the formal thought disorder, persecutory delusions, and acute presentation with clear stressor are more consistent with a primary psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Brief psychotic disorder is a time-limited psychotic episode lasting 1 day to 1 month with full return to premorbid functioning. It can occur with or without a marked stressor. The acute onset, often in response to severe psychosocial stress, and the absence of prodromal symptoms distinguish it from schizophrenia spectrum disorders. Treatment involves short-term low-dose antipsychotic medication for symptom control, with benzodiazepines for agitation and insomnia. Most patients recover fully, though approximately 20% may eventually meet criteria for a longer-duration psychotic disorder on follow-up.
key_points: Duration is 1 day to 1 month with full return to premorbid function
Can be specified as with or without a marked stressor
Short-term antipsychotic treatment is typically sufficient
Approximately 80% of patients recover fully without recurrence
If symptoms persist beyond 1 month the diagnosis is revised to schizophreniform disorder
explanation.pearls: Approximately 20% of patients initially diagnosed with brief psychotic disorder are eventually rediagnosed with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder on longitudinal follow-up - good prognostic features include acute onset, identifiable psychosocial stressor, preserved premorbid functioning, and absence of flat affect.

subtopic_detail: Schizophreniform disorder management and prognostic features
stem: A 22-year-old male university student is brought by his parents for psychiatric review. Over the past 3 months he has developed persecutory delusions that his lecturers are conspiring against him, auditory hallucinations of derogatory voices, and social withdrawal. His academic performance has declined markedly. He had no psychiatric symptoms before this episode and had been performing well academically. There is no substance use history. Physical examination and investigations are normal.

data_table.Examination: Flat affect, guarded, persecutory delusions (believes lecturers are sabotaging his grades), auditory hallucinations (second-person derogatory), social withdrawal for 3 months, no insight into illness
data_table.Investigations: UDS negative, TFTs normal, FBC and UEC normal, MRI brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnosis and management approach at this stage?

options.text: First-episode schizophrenia - commence antipsychotic with plan for indefinite treatment
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires at least 6 months of disturbance, including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms. At 3 months, this patient meets duration criteria for schizophreniform disorder but not yet schizophrenia. Presumptively diagnosing schizophrenia and committing to indefinite treatment is premature. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder - expect spontaneous resolution and avoid antipsychotic medication
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms lasting less than 1 month. This patient has had symptoms for 3 months, exceeding the 1-month limit. Additionally, the presence of negative symptoms (flat affect, social withdrawal) and the duration make this diagnosis inappropriate. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder with comorbid depression causing the functional decline and psychotic features
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires absence of other prominent psychotic symptoms beyond the delusion itself. This patient has auditory hallucinations, flat affect, and social withdrawal in addition to delusions, which goes beyond the isolated delusion pattern of delusional disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder - commence antipsychotic and reassess diagnosis at 6 months
options.rationale: Schizophreniform disorder is diagnosed when the full criteria for schizophrenia are met (at least two criterion A symptoms for at least 1 month) but the total duration of illness is less than 6 months. Treatment involves antipsychotic medication similar to first-episode schizophrenia. The diagnosis should be reviewed at 6 months: if symptoms persist, the diagnosis is changed to schizophrenia; if remission occurs, schizophreniform disorder is confirmed. Approximately 60% of patients with schizophreniform disorder eventually meet criteria for schizophrenia. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder despite negative drug screen, requiring repeat testing
options.rationale: The urine drug screen is negative and there is no substance use history. A 3-month course of symptoms with progressive negative symptoms and functional decline is not consistent with substance-induced psychosis, which typically resolves within weeks of cessation. Repeat testing without clinical indication is not warranted. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizophreniform disorder occupies a diagnostic space between brief psychotic disorder and schizophrenia, with a duration of at least 1 month but less than 6 months. It requires at least two criterion A schizophrenia symptoms. Treatment mirrors first-episode schizophrenia with low-dose second-generation antipsychotic medication and psychosocial support. The diagnosis is provisional and should be reviewed at 6 months. Good prognostic features include acute onset, confusion at the height of the psychotic episode, good premorbid functioning, and absence of blunted affect.
key_points: Schizophreniform disorder lasts at least 1 month but less than 6 months
Requires the same criterion A symptoms as schizophrenia
Approximately 60% of cases eventually progress to a schizophrenia diagnosis
Good prognostic features include acute onset and preserved premorbid functioning
Treatment is similar to first-episode schizophrenia with planned diagnostic review at 6 months
explanation.pearls: The provisional specifier in schizophreniform disorder allows clinicians to initiate appropriate treatment without prematurely committing to a schizophrenia diagnosis - the 6-month reassessment point is the key decision node where approximately 40% achieve full remission and 60% transition to schizophrenia.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective depressive type vs MDD with psychotic features discrimination
stem: A 36-year-old woman has a 7-year psychiatric history with multiple hospitalisations. During her first admission she presented with persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations for 3 months without any mood symptoms, which resolved with risperidone. Since then she has had three major depressive episodes, each lasting 2 to 4 months, during which her psychotic symptoms returned alongside severe depressed mood, anhedonia, and suicidal ideation. Between mood episodes she continues to experience intermittent auditory hallucinations and paranoid ideation despite euthymic mood.

data_table.Current presentation: Depressed mood for 6 weeks with psychotic symptoms, PHQ-9 22, continues to hear derogatory voices outside of mood episodes
data_table.Medications: Risperidone 4 mg daily, sertraline 150 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most accurate diagnosis based on the longitudinal course?

options.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features and inter-episode residual symptoms
options.rationale: In MDD with psychotic features, psychotic symptoms occur exclusively during depressive episodes and resolve with mood recovery. This patient has psychotic symptoms (hallucinations and paranoia) that persist during euthymic periods between mood episodes, which is inconsistent with MDD with psychotic features and points to schizoaffective disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid recurrent major depressive disorder as a separate diagnosis
options.rationale: While this patient has persistent psychotic symptoms, the mood episodes are prominent, prolonged (2 to 4 months each), and constitute a major proportion of the total illness duration. Schizoaffective disorder is the appropriate diagnosis when major mood episodes are present for a majority of the total illness duration in the setting of persistent psychotic symptoms. Schizophrenia with comorbid MDD would be considered only if mood symptoms were brief relative to psychosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features occurring during depressive episodes only
options.rationale: Bipolar I disorder requires at least one manic episode. This patient has had depressive episodes only, with no history of mania or hypomania. Additionally, bipolar disorder with psychotic features would not explain the persistent psychotic symptoms during euthymic periods. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Recurrent brief psychotic disorder occurring alongside major depressive episodes
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder episodes last less than 1 month and result in full return to premorbid functioning. This patient has persistent psychotic symptoms between mood episodes, which is not consistent with brief psychotic disorder. The longitudinal course requires a single integrated diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, depressive type, with psychotic symptoms persisting outside mood episodes
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires: (A) an uninterrupted period with a major mood episode concurrent with criterion A schizophrenia symptoms; (B) delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks in the ABSENCE of a major mood episode during the lifetime duration of illness; and (C) major mood episodes present for a majority of the total illness duration. This patient meets all criteria: concurrent psychosis and depression during episodes, persistent psychotic symptoms during euthymic periods (criterion B), and depressive episodes comprising a substantial proportion of her 7-year illness. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is one of the most challenging diagnoses in psychiatry, requiring careful longitudinal assessment. The key discriminating features are: psychotic symptoms must persist for at least 2 weeks in the absence of mood episodes (distinguishing it from MDD with psychotic features), and mood episodes must be present for a majority of the illness duration (distinguishing it from schizophrenia with comorbid mood symptoms). The depressive type is specified when the mood episodes are exclusively depressive. Treatment typically requires both antipsychotic and antidepressant medications.
key_points: Psychotic symptoms must persist for at least 2 weeks without concurrent mood episodes
Mood episodes must be present for a majority of the total illness duration
MDD with psychotic features has psychosis ONLY during mood episodes
Schizophrenia is diagnosed when mood episodes are brief relative to total illness
Treatment combines antipsychotic with antidepressant or mood stabiliser
explanation.pearls: The DSM-5-TR criterion B for schizoaffective disorder - delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode - is the single most important discriminator from mood disorder with psychotic features, and requires longitudinal assessment rather than cross-sectional diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective bipolar type vs bipolar I with psychotic features discrimination
stem: A 29-year-old man has a 5-year psychiatric history. His illness began with a 4-week manic episode with grandiose delusions and auditory hallucinations, which resolved with olanzapine and sodium valproate. Over the following 5 years he has had two further manic episodes and one major depressive episode, each with psychotic features. Between mood episodes he is euthymic with no psychotic symptoms. Collateral from his family confirms he has no delusions, hallucinations, or disorganised behaviour during well periods.

data_table.Current presentation: Euthymic, YMRS 2, PHQ-9 3, no psychotic symptoms currently
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 15 mg nocte, sodium valproate 1000 mg BD (level 72 mg/L)

lead_in: What is the most accurate diagnosis based on the longitudinal history?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features confined to mood episodes only
options.rationale: The distinguishing feature from schizoaffective disorder is that this patient has NO psychotic symptoms during euthymic periods. All psychotic features occur exclusively during manic or depressive episodes and resolve with mood recovery. The DSM-5-TR criterion B for schizoaffective disorder requires at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of a mood episode, which is not met here. This is bipolar I disorder with psychotic features. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, given the recurrent psychotic episodes over 5 years
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms persisting for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a mood episode during the lifetime illness. Collateral confirms this patient has no psychotic symptoms between mood episodes. Recurrence of psychosis with mood episodes alone does not meet schizoaffective criteria. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizophrenia with prominent affective symptoms as a comorbid feature of the illness
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires persistent psychotic symptoms, and mood episodes should be brief relative to total illness duration. This patient has psychosis only during discrete mood episodes with clear euthymic intervals, which is the opposite pattern. The mood episodes are the primary driver of psychotic symptoms. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Cyclothymic disorder with psychotic exacerbations during hypomanic upswings
options.rationale: Cyclothymic disorder involves chronic fluctuating hypomanic and depressive symptoms that never meet full criteria for manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episodes. This patient has had full manic episodes meeting diagnostic criteria, which excludes cyclothymia. Additionally, cyclothymia does not include psychotic features. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Recurrent brief psychotic episodes triggered by mood instability without a primary mood disorder
options.rationale: The psychotic episodes occur in the context of full manic and depressive episodes meeting DSM-5-TR criteria, not as independent brief psychotic events. The mood disorder is the primary diagnosis with psychotic features as a severity specifier. Brief psychotic disorder would not explain the recurrent mood episodes. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: The distinction between bipolar I with psychotic features and schizoaffective disorder bipolar type is one of the most clinically important differential diagnoses in psychiatry. The key discriminator is whether psychotic symptoms occur ONLY during mood episodes (bipolar I with psychotic features) or persist for at least 2 weeks in the absence of mood episodes (schizoaffective disorder). This distinction requires careful longitudinal assessment with collateral history, as cross-sectional evaluation during a mood episode cannot differentiate the two. The treatment implications are significant: bipolar I is managed primarily with mood stabilisers, while schizoaffective disorder typically requires ongoing antipsychotic medication.
key_points: Bipolar I with psychotic features has psychosis ONLY during mood episodes
Schizoaffective bipolar type has psychosis persisting outside mood episodes for at least 2 weeks
Longitudinal assessment with collateral history is essential for accurate diagnosis
Cross-sectional evaluation during a mood episode cannot distinguish the two disorders
Treatment implications differ: mood stabiliser-centred vs antipsychotic-centred maintenance
explanation.pearls: In practice, the most reliable way to distinguish bipolar I with psychotic features from schizoaffective disorder is to interview the patient and collateral informants during a euthymic period - if psychotic symptoms are completely absent during sustained euthymia, schizoaffective disorder is excluded.

subtopic_detail: Substance-induced depressive disorder vs MDD with comorbid substance use
stem: A 42-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, poor concentration, and passive suicidal ideation. He has been drinking heavily (approximately 80 g of alcohol per day) for the past 2 months following a relationship breakdown. He has no prior psychiatric history. He reports his mood was entirely normal before the heavy drinking began. He is assessed 5 days after his last drink, having been admitted for alcohol withdrawal management.

data_table.Examination: PHQ-9 18, no psychotic features, passive suicidal ideation without plan, mildly anxious, GGT 286 U/L, MCV 102 fL, BAL 0 (5 days abstinent)
data_table.Investigations: TFTs normal, B12 and folate normal, UDS negative apart from alcohol metabolites

lead_in: What is the most appropriate approach to his depressive symptoms at this stage?

options.text: Diagnose MDD and commence sertraline 50 mg daily immediately alongside alcohol treatment
options.rationale: The temporal relationship between heavy alcohol use and depressive symptoms, combined with no prior psychiatric history, raises strong suspicion for substance-induced depressive disorder. Commencing antidepressants before allowing adequate abstinence risks treating a pharmacologically induced mood state that may resolve spontaneously. Antidepressants also have reduced efficacy during active heavy drinking. (Source: eTG: Psychotropic; RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Monitor mood during 2 to 4 weeks of sustained abstinence before diagnosing a primary mood disorder
options.rationale: When depressive symptoms develop in the context of heavy substance use and there is no prior psychiatric history, the recommended approach is to establish abstinence and reassess mood after 2 to 4 weeks. Substance-induced depressive disorder typically improves significantly with sustained abstinence. If symptoms persist beyond this period, a primary mood disorder is more likely and antidepressant treatment should be initiated. During the observation period, supportive care and safety monitoring are essential. (Source: DSM-5-TR; eTG: Psychotropic; RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Diagnose substance-induced depressive disorder and reassure that no further treatment is required
options.rationale: While substance-induced depressive disorder is the working diagnosis, the patient has passive suicidal ideation and significant functional impairment requiring active monitoring and support. Dismissive reassurance is inappropriate. He needs close follow-up, safety planning, and mood reassessment after a period of abstinence. Supportive therapy should be offered regardless of diagnostic certainty. (Source: eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence mirtazapine for the dual benefit of improving mood and promoting sleep during recovery
options.rationale: While mirtazapine's sedating properties may seem helpful, prescribing an antidepressant before establishing whether the depression is substance-induced is premature. If the depression resolves with abstinence, unnecessary medication exposure and potential side effects are avoided. The 2 to 4 week observation period during abstinence is the recommended first step. (Source: eTG: Psychotropic; RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Commence naltrexone 50 mg daily for alcohol use disorder as it will also treat the depressive symptoms
options.rationale: Naltrexone is an effective relapse prevention medication for alcohol use disorder but has no direct antidepressant effect. While treating the alcohol use disorder is important and naltrexone should be considered, it will not address depressive symptoms independently. The mood assessment approach should follow the observation protocol regardless of AUD treatment. (Source: AMH; eTG: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Distinguishing substance-induced depressive disorder from primary MDD with comorbid substance use is a common clinical challenge. The DSM-5-TR diagnostic approach relies on temporal relationships: if mood symptoms develop during or within 1 month of substance intoxication or withdrawal, substance-induced disorder is the presumptive diagnosis. The key management principle is to establish abstinence and reassess mood after 2 to 4 weeks. If depressive symptoms persist beyond this period despite sustained abstinence, a primary mood disorder is diagnosed and antidepressant treatment is initiated.
key_points: Temporal relationship between substance use and mood onset is the key diagnostic clue
Observe mood during 2 to 4 weeks of sustained abstinence before diagnosing primary MDD
Substance-induced depressive disorder typically improves with abstinence alone
Active safety monitoring is required during the observation period especially with suicidal ideation
Antidepressants have reduced efficacy during active heavy alcohol use
explanation.pearls: Approximately 40% of depressive episodes that begin during heavy alcohol use resolve within 2 to 4 weeks of sustained abstinence, confirming a substance-induced aetiology - this proportion rises to over 60% when there is no prior psychiatric history, reinforcing the value of the abstinence observation period.
